sentences.text: A few days later, we were assigned to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
But from the time that we heard what that smoke is that-- that the blockalteste told us about, I could not get over the shock.
How could there be a death factory?
How could there be such a place?
We thought that she was just trying to be cruel.
We-- we thought that she was just jealous of us, because we just came, and we're so happy.
God knows how long she's been there already.
And she was deprived of her freedom for so long.
I don't know what the background is, but I don't think I'm so far off when I- when I think that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She, however, opened my eyes as I've never seen them opened before.
Indeed, she was right.
We were in a death factory.
The work where we were assigned to-- mother, my sister, and I-- was called the Kanada work detail.

We would march from our barrack in Birkenau out the electric wire fences-- the-- the gate rather-- attached to the electric wire fences, into the-- that narrow corridor that took us down to the forested area.
And there we would enter another electric wire fence gate.
And that is the area of the gas chambers.
Four gas chambers operated around the clock around us.
We were in the center.
That is where Kanada work detail is or was.
And we would see endless columns enter these buildings and never come out.
I worked directly behind gas chamber and crematorium, now known as number four.
And it did not have an undressing dressing room.
And so we would see the people change, or get undressed-- not change, but get undressed-- in front of the gas chamber and crematorium on the beautiful green lawn, as we passed by them to go to work.
We just passed them by, and made a left turn on the other side of the gas chamber and crematorium, and just a few feet, and we were at our work place.
And there we sorted-- sorted out the clothes, bundles, and packages that they all-- that we all brought from home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes this would be an advantage to us, because once in a while we would find food in these packages.
And we would supplement our meager diet with these-- with this food.
Only then would our calories increase, but we were not permitted to eat any of it.
And so we had to do it very discreetly.
But when we found something edible, we found a way.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- it was an unbelievable place.
People would bring along their best clothes, usually the transports from Hungary, they were the ones that were coming in the next few months.
And we were-- we were asked to look in the lining of the clothes for hidden jewelry.
And so we had to feel the shoulder pads, which people wore at that time, and the linings.
And we very often found diamonds.
People brought along small things, small items that could be easily carried.
And they brought-- they probably-- it probably-- they probably were their lifetime savings.
Who knows?
But this is what they were hoping to save their lives with, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] possibly.
And it all went into a different pile.
There were these different piles.
There were piles of shoes, masses of hair, luggage, jewelry, wedding bands.
Can you imagine a pile of wedding bands, each with its own sentimental attachment to it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
One time my mother found a sizable diamond.
I forget whether it was sewn in a coat-- into a coat, or into a lining, or whether it was in-- in a-- in a piece of luggage.
I forget that part, but mother said, if we ever get out of this place, what are we going to live off of?
So I said, mother, there is always the land.
We did it since 1938 until 1944.
She said, yes, but you need other things.
Children grow their-- grow out their clothes.
We'll need shoes.
We'll need dental care, medical care.
Says we need a lot of money to run a household, honey.
So, of course, she knew-- I didn't know.
And she said, I found a diamond, and I'm going to put it in my shoe.
Because she too-- we were all able to keep our shoes in our transport.
And she had shoes that went up to just under your knees and with-- laced up.
It's very much in style right now, as a matter of fact.
They were brown and leather lined as well.
And mother took it off and put it into the toe area, wrapped up in a little piece of paper.
And I used to kid her that you're going to have all sorts of blisters on your toes.
Is it really worth it?
And then I really became scared that mother is likely to lose her life on account of that diamond.
And maybe Anushka and I too, because they tended to disrupt families wherever they could.
And so we had to sort of keep a low profile that she-- that she's my mother, and Anushka is my sister.
But we were-- we had a Hungarian guard, who sometimes spoke Hungarian to us.
And this was a blessing, because once in a while he-- he would tell us that he came from Hungary and that he has a grandchild.
And he would take his wallet out and show his grandchild to us.
And he sort of held it out like that.
And-- but he couldn't talk just any time.
This really was very rare, but once in a while he did.
And Anushka would take some soap, if she found soap-- a bar of soap-- she would take it and put it in his pocket.
And he didn't say yes, he didn't say no, he didn't say thank you.
He just stood there and just sort of smiled a little.
And mother encouraged Anushka to do this when she finds something that was hard to get during war time, and that he-- something that he would appreciate, and something small.
And so there were other things that she put into his pocket.
And now this guard turned out to be very helpful later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to just tell you a few incidents that happened in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As you know, each area was surrounded by tall electric wire fences, at least twice as tall as an average human being.
Near the Kanada work detail, the very next area, surrounded by electric wire fences, of course, was the twin lager.
And there were twins with bandages on, and all sorts of-- with all sorts of problems.
And once in a while, mother would wrap around-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find something and wrap it around with some cloth, some kind of clothes, and weigh it down with a piece of stone.
There was-- there was gravel, sort of rough stone-like gravel all around.

And she would throw the-- this package across the electric wire fence into the twin lager, the twin camps, where they experimented on-- on-- where the-- these were girls and boys on whom they experimented on.
Actually, I don't remember boys.
I only remember girls.
So maybe they kept the boys elsewhere.
And they really were very thin and emaciated.
And mother wanted to help out.
She did this a few times, and then she was caught.
And she was taken to the lager kommandant by the kapo this time, by the kapo.
And luckily-- luckily for my mother, the kapo liked her-- liked my mother-- and knew that there was a very young [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] daughter that works also under her command.
And but took my mother to the kapo and said that this woman threw a-- a package over to the twin lager, and she should be-- she should be reprimanded.
And-- and he barked I'll decide what has to be done with her.
And he was going to tell her how many lashes that she should get when she didn't permit him to say this.
And instead she added, before he could say anything, this woman's young girl, is my very best worker, herr kommandant, and if I lose her, I'm going to lose her daughter too.
And so he got the message.
But I suppose sometimes they listened to the kapos.
And so mother was given a strong reprimand, that if she'll ever do this again, she will never come out alive from what she will receive as a result.
And so mother gratefully was returned to Anushka and me.
I was there, too, but the kapo didn't mention me.
This was-- there was a reason for it, I'm sure.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Oh, thank you.
I know I started that, and I overlooked that story.
One day, we had to remove all our clothes after we worked.
It was a total surprise.
That-- this was the first time-- and-- for an inspection.
Shoes, everything, had to be removed.
And so, well, we didn't have much clothes on.
We had a dress on and the shoes.
And so we had to remove the dress.
We had to remove his shoes.
So we were really-- this time we were totally naked.
And I got-- I got so worried, and mother came over and, looking some-- in another direction, said, please pretend you know nothing about the diamond.
I said, mother, you're going to have to get rid of it, or-- or we're going to lose you.
And she-- she said, I will-- I will do it my way.
And so she picked up a-- a-- actually didn't pick it up, but with the toes of her shoes, before she took it off, she created a little hole there.
And then she took off her shoe, that shoe, the right shoe, and she turned it upside down and sort of dropped it.
And while she was removing her other shoe, she moved the pebble over the diamond.
And I really took a-- I did a sigh of relief.

Anushka and I were-- she especially-- was very scared for mother's life, because by this time we-- we saw the sort of things that the SS were capable of doing, how quickly a human life ended, just brutally, under the whip of the SS.
This-- I don't think I could bear seeing that, and my mother being-- become the victim.
No diamond is worth that.
And it's really incredible.
It's not that she just-- it has nothing to do with wanting to become wealthy, or-- but it had-- she already thought about the time when we will be free.
And she wanted to be able to provide for us, as if she expected that we may have nothing left at home, if we ever get out of this hell hole.
So that was that incident.
We passed it, of course, with flying colors.
We worked in this Kanada work detail 'til October.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And October, I think, the 15th, when the-- something happened.
There was a kommando of women who worked outside of the perimeter of Auschwitz.
This is Auschwitz to Birkenau.
And every day, they went out, they worked with-- with gunpowder.
And every day they were searched for gunpowder, because it could be obviously detrimental to the SS to-- to have-- to-- to-- for us to have gunpowder.
And what I didn't know at that time, that there was an underground.
I suppose I was too young to be asked to be in the underground.
But there was an active underground in Auschwitz.
And every day, the women who worked in this gunpowder-- with gunpowder in the factory-- they smuggled out-- out pinches of gunpowder in very special crevices in their bodies.
And they turned it over to a-- an individual who was a male, and who was a member of the underground.
And these people, these men, were part of the sonderkommando.
The sonderkommando is a German term for a group of men who did nothing but took care of the bodies once they were gassed.
They-- once these people were gassed, they had to take the bodies out on wheelbarrow type of vehicles, and they-- they took them into the crematorium and burned the bodies there.
And so the-- the-- the death process is what they were assigned to do.
It's that or die.

One day, the sonderkommando-- and I would really prefer to call them the underground in this case, for there may have been others involved, and I-- I don't know-- blew up gas chamber and crematorium number four.

And a few days later, the-- the news reached us by way of the grapevine that the reason we no longer can work in gas chamber and crematorium number four is because it was blown up and put out of action.
Thousands and thousands of men, women, and children were able to save their lives as a result of this action.
We-- we were jubilant.

We were jubilant, and why couldn't this be done before and-- and elsewhere, hopefully, if-- we-- actually, when we were in Auschwitz, we had no idea that there were other camps until there were others who were transferred into our camp.
And then we learned that this one came from this camp, and that one came from that, and so forth and so on.
And we said, yet another camp?
Maybe four or five or six camps?
Little did I know at the time that there were thousands of camps, concentration camps, a whole network of concentration camps throughout Germany.
What happened to the sonderkommando who did this act?
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I first was transferred to another job with my mother and my sister.
And that was called the [SPEAKING GERMAN] The [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It's interesting that nobody seems to know about the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And many years later, my sister and I talked about the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It really means weavery, or a place where one weaves, in German, but our job there was to process the clothes that was not fit to be sent to Germany.
For what we did with the clothes in the Kanada work detail, was we packaged them for shipment to Germany.
Yes, the German folk wore our clothes from home during World War II.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, the clothes that were not fit to be worn, apparently, was cut up in-- into strips about this wide and-- somewhere else, not where I worked.
Maybe in another barrack nearby.
And somebody sewed up the ends.
But by the time these strips reached us, they were just piles of-- heaps of-- of these things.
We had to twirl them, and we had to braid them, just like hair.
We braid-- we braided them.
And little do I know what they used them for.
I have since done some research, and there were two theories.
And one was that they were used on German submarines during World War II, in order to seal the open areas, areas that can open up.
And also that they were used for fuse on-- out in the front, in the battlefield.
So whether this is correct or not, I'm not certain.
But we kept making these huge balls of braided work.
One time, something happened at the [SPEAKING GERMAN] that really scared us.
An SS showed up with his helper and a whip made of leather in his hand.
And he wore a white glove.
It was not Dr.
Josef Mengele, the camp doctor of Auschwitz.
It was another SS.
And he said to my sister, [SPEAKING GERMAN], come here.
And my sister almost fainted, she said.
She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was told that, OK, I want to see how fast you braid your work.
And he said start.
When I tell you start, you start.
And you keep doing it until I stop the clock, until I tell you.
And so said, now, and continued to do it for about two, three minutes, I don't know.
And says stop, and she stopped.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] measured the length of her work, and was very pleased.
And he announced to the rest of us, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, you dirty Jews.
This little girl can do this much during-- during this two or three minutes, whatever the time was.
I want to see all of you do this.
If you come short of that-- that you will not see daylight again.
And that's-- that's just what happened.
Then he tested the rest of us.
When this was all over, my sister was led away by the SS.
And we understood to mean that whoever will not do enough of the rest of us, will be taken away and never see daylight again.
And here, only my sister was taken away.
And mother started crying, and we had no idea where she went, where she was taken to.
And we had to continue to work.
And in about 20 minutes or so, here she comes, and here she is holding a bunch of stuff in her arms, smiling, and laughing.
And I couldn't believe it.
Food.
She was taken to a place-- I don't know where there was such a place in Auschwitz.
We never saw it.
And she was given soda water and herring and something else-- cheese.
And I don't know what else.
Bread.
And she-- she had to carry it like this in her arms.
Where did you get that, mother said.
She says there's a little canteen down the road for the SS.
And-- and then he just took everything he saw, and he-- until there was no more room in my arms.
And can you imagine?
In the meantime, we lost quite a few women among the rest of us, who were taken away because our-- because their strip was not as long as my sister's.
So they pulled all sorts of stunts to thin us out.
For example, periodically, Dr.
Mengele would appear, and we would have to-- on the pretext of a medical check up-- we would have to remove all our clothes, and in our birthday suits, just sometimes we were asked to turn around.
And before you were sent either this way or that way.
And one of those-- he decided who should live and who should die, who is no longer in-- in a shape to go on, just based on what he saw from this selection.
Well, one time I was picked out and sent to the infirmary, because there were some what looked like rashes under my pubic hair.
Well, it was in the summer.
I don't know which month it was, but it was very, very hot.
And they turned off the water.
They turned it off for several days just to thin us out.
And we had absolutely no water, and those were the hottest days that-- that I experienced in Auschwitz.
And we were dehydrated.
And the dust was all over.
We couldn't even have a little rain to at least wash our faces in a puddle.
It was indescribable.
We-- I-- I didn't take a bath.
For 10 months from the time that I arrived in Auschwitz, I did not have a-- a bath.
And we started having lice in Auschwitz already.
But I will get to that later.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I forgot where I was.
Then Mengele came.
Oh, yes.
And we had to undress on the pretext of a medical check up.
Dr.
Mengele asked me to turn around.
He noticed these pimples, and-- and sent me over there.
Not a word was uttered ever.
I had-- I don't think I heard his voice.
I just-- he just pointed, this way or that way.
And so it was that I was sent to the other side, and Dr.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mengele didn't send me to death, but sent me to the infirmary to heal.
